speaker: interviewer
text: Tape four, Aaron Elster. You talked about your succeeding through fear of failure and your feeling that you didn't deserve the success that you've achieved, and gradually being able to work more toward being able to accept that. Where do you think some of those things come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it comes from my background. It comes from my childhood. It comes from living a certain way and being told that I'm less of a person, and being put upon, and it stays with you. It's hard to get over certain things that you pick up as a child. It was much easier for me to accept a certain way of life than an easy way of life, because the easy way of life, for some odd reason, I wasn't convinced till just recently that I deserve it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why? How? I-- I really-- I'm not that deep that I can give you that kind of answer, but it-- it stems from my childhood. I'm able to rationalize things now more so. I'm able to forgive myself more so now. I'm also able to [POLISH]-- if you know what that means-- to not begrudge myself if something good happens to me at times-- at times, because I've come to the conclusion that, you know, why not me? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So but we've all been affected by that, and we'll probably never totally resolve our feelings or our situation. And you know what? I really don't feel bad about holding on or hanging on to some of my pains and some of my inhibitions because they-- they act as a check for me just in case I get carried away with myself. It's always there to-- to hold me in place. And it's served me-- I think it's served me well.

speaker: interviewee
text: And further, I don't want to totally give it up. It would take away from the meaning of my life. That's the way I believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think the meaning of your life is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes-- if you-- if you catch me at certain times, I'll tell you that we or I am a joke in God's eyes. He created us, and then as soon as we are born, we get ready to die. We have a certain period, a short period of time, in between, and when you really have all your senses and you should live, you start diminishing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I don't know. I think it's-- at times, I think it's all a joke. And at times I think that we serve a purpose. And my purpose in life is to have my children carry on, and my grandchildren carry on, to try and help create a better world. So it depends on the mood I'm in. It depends what's happening with me. I'm not saying that I'm right. I just-- this is the way I feel. I've been preoccupied with dying ever since I was a kid, even in good times, and wondering what it's going to be like. What is there after? If you remember Peggy Lee's song 'Is That All There Is?' I don't know if that's all there is. I'd like to come back and tell somebody, but I don't think I'll be able to. But anyway, that's-- that's how I feel. Do you think about your Holocaust experiences often?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tried to avoid them for many years, to a point where I think I went too far. I associate a certain guilt with the Holocaust. I was not too proud of certain things that were happening, not because of choice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what kinds of things?

speaker: interviewee
text: People going to their death. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Not in my own childish mind could I understand why people would succumb to that. I understand it now. I know-- I know the reasons now, but I had trouble with that. I'll give you an example. If you line up 100 people against the wall, and I have a gun and I only have one bullet, I can control 100 people. Because 1 of 100 I'm going to kill, and you don't want to be that one out of the 100. So it's easy to have that kind of power. Plus, our people were brought up in a Jewish tradition, in a religious tradition, and God will provide, and God will help, and if somebody slaps you on one side of the cheek, give them the other side of the cheek. It's only of recent history that the Jews have begun to say, never again. And it's because of Israel that that has happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I tried to shed my Holocaust or my survivor image. I wanted to mingle. I wanted to fit in. I wanted to be an American. Because if people would ask me my story, I would have mixed emotions. Do they want to hear it because they care? Is it a mere curiosity? And there are certain things I'm going to say they're not going to believe. And you get to a point and you say, what's the point, OK? So you know, I never knew, but I did terribly want to belong. So therefore, when I started growing up and when I was an adult, I didn't want to inflict this type of baggage on my children. And as I said, I think I've gone overboard, although it turned out all right. They didn't have any religious upbringing at all, and I'm sorry for that now, because if I don't expose them to religion, I can't really expect them at age 18 or 20 to suddenly turn religious. So my philosophy was let them make a choice, but I didn't give them a choice. I made a choice for them, and I realize it now where I didn't realize it before, OK? Has your belief in God changed due to your Holocaust experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: I feel guilty about my belief in God because I still go to God and ask him to help me when I need help. And then when I-- when things are normal, I put God in the background, and I feel hypocritical. I feel very bad about that. I envy religious people. I do. I wish I could have a total belief. I would be a much happier person.

speaker: interviewer
text: But there's something in me that I-- too scared to say that it doesn't exist, because what if it does? I don't want to wind up in hell for eternity, OK? But I have a problem with it, and we've tried to rationalize it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody said to me that God doesn't really control everything-- he guides things. I-- I-- I-- this was with a rabbi, and I have trouble with that. And then some rabbis will tell you that it was meant to be that way, and I, oh-- I just wanna-- I go out of my skin when somebody says that to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: That it was meant to be that way? That Jews were supposed to be punished like that? That children were supposed to be killed and burned? And my little sister, six years old, who did she hurt? I have tremendous problems with that, so I don't know whether I'll ever resolve it or not. Why do you think you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I'll tell you, I think it was just a quirk. I don't think there was any special design. I don't think it was in God's design that I should survive, especially after I told you how I felt about myself. There were so many more pious people or pious children that should have survived. Why me? So I don't think it was a grand design. I think it was just--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think you had anything to do with it? Or was it pure luck?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. I helped the-- I helped the situation, but it could have gone the other way. Just-- it could have gone any-- any other way at all. So why? I wish I knew. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I did-- I do know that I didn't want to die, and I fought not to die, and maybe that helped. They're going to drag me out of here. I'm not going easily. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I do feel, though, I have an obligation now, suddenly. In my-- in my later years in life, I have an obligation to tell the story. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have an obligation to participate in Holocaust affairs. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have an obligation to carry on because there are so few of us left now. And there's so many people out there saying that it never happened. And 40, 50 years from now, 10, 15 years from now, there ain't-- there ain't going to be too many of us left.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the story has to be told for historical reasons, if for no other reason at all. I'm not looking for anybody's pity or understanding at this point. It's beyond that point. I'm waiting for my grandchildren to get to a certain age. I want them to know the story. I will want them to watch this tape. What would you hope they would get out of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I will want them to know. I will want them to know where they came from, their history. It's-- it's-- they should. They must. And if they're understanding children, they will carry that on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you feel it would give them to know all that?

speaker: interviewee
text: An appreciation for life. Not to take life for granted. Not to ever think that it cannot happen here. It can happen anyplace. It's happening right now all over the world. Take a look at Bosnia, OK? It would give them an appreciation for life. It would give them an understanding. It'd also maybe teach them some tolerance of other people, which, you know, we-- we-- we don't have a patent on that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you'd like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I've said it. I-- I have a closer feeling for my Holocaust colleagues now than I ever did. I feel proud of being one of them. I feel proud of some of the accomplishments that they have done. So I have a totally different feeling than I had many years ago. And it's OK to have an accent. I've accepted it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much--

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: For sharing your story with us. Greatly appreciate it.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my-- this is a picture of my grandfather, Mordechai Scherb, my grandmother, Masha, and my aunt, Malka-- my mother's sister and my mother's parents. This picture was probably taking-- taken about 1930, '31. My grandfather and grandmother died in Treblinka. My-- my aunt escaped to Russia and she survived. She recently passed away at an old age in Israel. This is a picture of my mother. This picture was taken 1940-- 1939, 1940. As a kid, I thought she was the most beautiful woman in the world. She died in 1944, the end of '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Cywia. Cywia Scherb Elster. This is a picture taken of my mother, my little sister, Sara, and her cousin. If you notice, she's wearing an armband, and that was during the ghetto. You had to wear an armband with the Star of David. My little sister Sara died in Treblinka in 1942. My granddaughter is named after her. OK, this is a picture of myself on the right, my sister on the left, and my hero, my Uncle Sam, in the middle. He's the one that rescued us out of Poland, smuggled us across to Czechoslovakia, and from Czechoslovakia to Germany. He's alive, he's in his mid-70s. He lives in New York right now. Great affection for him. He really helped us a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: About when with this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1945, Right after liberation. This is a picture of me in 1945 in Lodz. We just left Sokolow. We went to Lodz. I lived in a-- an orphanage, came back to Lodz, and this is-- this is what I looked like. This picture was taken in Germany, [GERMAN ] I was going to school. I was on a soccer team. I thought I was going to take after my Uncle Sam, but unfortunately, I never really made it. But it was in 1946. This is a graduation-- high school graduation picture of myself. 1953, I was approximately 19 years of age. And at those years and those times, I thought I was Tony Curtis or John Derek. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This picture was taken in Korea near Seoul-- [KOREAN], Korea in 1954. I was in the army. I was a supply sergeant at that time. I sent this picture home to my wife with a little inscription saying, 'To my-- to my sweet ketzelle. Love, Aaron.' To my sweet ketzelle, meaning my sweet kitten. This is my wedding picture taken in 1954. This is my wife, Jackie, gorgeous woman. This is a picture of my sister, Irene, myself, and my brother-in-law Jack, Jack Budkowski. Irene is the-- my sister that survived with me in hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken mid-'80s. This is my son Bob and his lady Marie. He lives out in California. He's the smart one in the family. He won't come back to Chicago because it's too cold. This was taken 1995. This is the love of my life, my wife and my two granddaughters, Sarah and Allison.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one's on which side?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sarah's on the right side, Allison's on the left side. And that picture was taken probably 1993. 1993. I'd like you to meet my family. This is my wife, Jackie. We've been married for 41 years. And this is my granddaughter, Allison and Sarah. They're the love of our lives. And in back of me is my sweet daughter, Claudia, my son, Steve.

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, we'd like to just say that we're very, very proud to be here tonight. The one thing that comes to my mind is how excited I am to see my father get so involved in what he's been doing with the organizations he's been involved in, and also the fact that he's been able to at least somehow, in a controlled manner, be able to discuss his experiences that he's had in the past. I've never been able to really realize the-- the anguish, the frustrations, that he's had. I'm 38 years old, and I'd say, probably within the last 5 years have I really understood what my father has gone through. And this, again, is another episode that has helped myself, and hopefully my family understand some of the past that my father has gone through.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I just wanted to say that we're all very proud of Aaron, and I'm very grateful that he's bringing his past into light now. And I think possibly it was the birth of his two granddaughters that may have flickered a little light in wanting to bring his past and his history into-- into better view, and we're proud of him for doing that. And we all love him very much, and we're-- he's a wonderful person.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: You guys want to say anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: You want to say you love your-- your papa?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wave and say hello.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's OK. That's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: There you go.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's OK. That's fine. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right, guys. The ordeal is over.
